attributes.stem:
A 41 year-old man presents with some lumps around his neck. They occur on both the left and the right and are painless but increasing in size. He has otherwise been well and has no medical history. On examination, there are enlarged supraclavicular lymph nodes bilaterally. The masses are firm with evidence of tethering. His thyroid is not enlarged (Image). His thyroid function tests are shown below:

  • TSH: 6.1mU/L² (0.4–4.0)

  • T3: 2.8pmol/L (3.5 – 7.8)

  • T4: 4.0pmol/L (9 – 24)

His thyroid ultrasound demonstrates microcalcifications. Biopsy of his thyroid with fine needle aspiration shows calcifications and overlapping nuclei with nuclear grooves.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has bilateral supraclavicular lymph node enlargement. While there is a long list of potential causes of lymph node enlargement, such as infections, primary cancers and metastases, the additional testing is most consistent with thyroid malignancy. This is demonstrated his ultrasound showing microcalcifications, and histological changes on biopsy indicative of a malignancy. Therefore, his lymphadenopathy is most likely from cancer metastasis with a previously undetected primary from his thyroid.

Of the possible options, which are all thyroid malignancies, papillary carcinoma is the most likely answer. Papillary carcinoma is the most common thyroid malignancy, and metastases in the lymph nodes are commonly detected prior to the primary tumour. This cancer shows microcalcifications on ultrasound, as well as calcifications (also referred to as Psammoma bodies) and has overlapping nuclei with nuclear grooves on histology. There may also be clear nuclei on histology (also referred to as Orphan Annie eyes).

Lymphoma of thyroid - this cancer is typically a complication of Hashimoto's thyroiditis. As this patient has no medical history, this diagnosis is less likely.

Medullary carcinoma - this is less likely as we would see considerably different findings on histology. In this cancer, amyloid deposits, punctate nuclei, granular cytoplasm, and/or round polygonal cells are expected.

Anaplastic carcinoma - this is a fast growing cancer that can also metastasise quickly, hence it is usually associated with dysphagia and dyspnoea. Poorly differentiated cells are seen on histology that have no resemblance to the original cells; this is not the case here.

Follicular carcinoma - this is a cancer that is difficult to diagnose on FNA and would usually require partial or complete thyroid removal to effectively diagnosis this cancer. However, this patient does not have a mass on his thyroid.
learningPoints: Papillary thyroid carcinoma is the most common form of thyroid malignancy, and palpable lymph nodes because of metastases are usually detected before the primary tumour.
Papillary carcinoma shows calcifications (Psammoma bodies), clear nuclei (Orphan-Annie eyes), and overlapping nuclei with nuclear grooves on histology.
references: Limaiem F, Rehman A, Anastasopoulou C, et al. Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma. [Updated 2023 Jan 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK536943/
Prete, A., Borges de Souza, P., Censi, S., Muzza, M., Nucci, N., & Sponziello, M. (2020). Update on fundamental mechanisms of thyroid cancer. Frontiers in endocrinology, 11, 102.

Lee K, Anastasopoulou C, Chandran C, et al. Thyroid Cancer. [Updated 2023 May 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459299/
